channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Inside the Katseye Fandom: Friendships, Rivalries and Boycotts Over Manon’s Hiatus
item.description: Fans of the group have leveraged their influence into friendship and rivalry. Then a member’s sudden hiatus sent them into a spiral.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai and Matt Yan
item.media:credit: Alexander Coggin for The New York Times
item.media:description: “You have to remind yourself that you don’t know them personally,” said Joy Lebofsky, who has experienced the joys and disappointments of being a stan.

item.title: As War With Iran Hurts Oil Prices, U.S. Turns to Iranian Boats for Help
item.description: Iranian-linked ships carrying Russian oil were among those that received temporary exemptions from sanctions, a sign of how dire the energy crisis is becoming.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A tanker in the Persian Gulf last week. Ships carrying Russian oil as of Thursday are temporarily exempt from U.S. sanctions.

item.title: Airport Security Lines Grow as TSA Goes Unpaid in Partial Shutdown
item.description: “There’s going to be a breaking point sooner or later,” one union official warned, with travelers at some airports being told to arrive three hours ahead of time.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: With Transportation Security Administration officers missing paychecks as the partial government shutdown enters its fifth week, long lines are beginning to form at major airports.

item.title: Judge Strikes Down RFK Jr.’s Vaccine Policies in Blow to Trump’s Health Agenda
item.description: Ruling on a lawsuit brought by several prominent medical organizations, a district court said the federal government had not based its decisions on science in limiting Covid shots and revising the childhood immunization schedule.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices in Atlanta in December.

item.title: Trump Officials Look to More Managed Approach to Trade With China
item.description: The administration has begun discussing a new “Board of Trade” with China to try to balance what U.S. officials see as an unequal economic relationship.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: Scott Bessent, the Treasury secretary, left, and Jamieson Greer, the U.S. trade representative, conducted talks with their Chinese counterparts on Sunday and Monday in Paris.

item.title: Nvidia Debuts New A.I. Product at GTC Developer Conference
item.description: At the opening of the company’s annual conference, Jensen Huang leaned on technology from a recent deal to show how artificial intelligence is changing.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang and Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nvidia chief executive Jensen Huang at the company’s GTC conference in San Jose on Monday.

item.title: With Threats and Claims of ‘Treason,’ Trump Pressures Media on the War
item.description: A president who calls journalists the “enemy of the people” has accused news organizations of undermining a military effort that many Americans oppose.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission, has referred to the news media as “fake news,” a phrase often invoked by President Trump.

item.title: With Iran War, Trump Risks Stepping on Gains From His Own Tax Cuts
item.description: President Trump’s war in Iran has raised some costs just as many Americans are starting to see savings from last year’s tax cuts.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The dominant impression of the economy for many Americans this spring could come down to rapidly rising gas prices.

item.title: Why Trump’s Strait of Hormuz Problem Is Going Global
item.description: President Trump is getting little to no support so far for his call that other countries do their part to try to ease an energy crisis prompted by U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Paralyzed ship traffic in the Strait of Hormuz continues to disrupt the global energy market as well as geopolitics.

item.title: Iran War Risks Sharpening Fed Divisions Over Rate Cuts
item.description: The Federal Reserve is set to hold interest rates steady this week as officials grapple with a host of new economic risks stemming from the conflict in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: For Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, the war in Iran is compounding the challenge of reaching a consensus among the central bank’s policymakers.

item.title: Rising Prices and High Interest Rates Are Making Car Ownership Feel Impossible
item.description: Rising vehicle prices, auto loan interest rates, and insurance and maintenance costs are making it harder for people to buy or keep cars.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Josh Ritchie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Davine Greene, 24, has not been able to keep up with her car payments and has had to file for bankruptcy.

item.title: Lululemon Scrambles to Revive Yoga Pants Empire Amid Fight With Founder
item.description: The interim chief executives are trying to rejuvenate sales as the founder, Chip Wilson, engages in a proxy battle for the boardroom.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Desean McClinton-Holland for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lululemon, a brand that defined an era of butt-hugging, stretchy leggings, has lost its sheen.

item.title: Oil Holds Above $100 a Barrel as Supply Worries Persist
item.description: As the broader war in the Middle East begins its third week, worries over the fallout of crippled energy supplies continue to roil markets.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The War Is Making It Harder to Keep the Lights On, 2,000 Miles Away
item.description: Bangladesh is taking steps to conserve electricity, which its factories need to keep stitching together the world’s clothing.
item.dc:creator: Saif Hasnat and Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fabeha Monir for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Bangladeshis are very good at doing one thing, like ready-made garments,” said Mohiuddin Rubel, who runs several garment factories. “We are not diversified.”

item.title: Best picture nominees had mixed box office firepower.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Oscars 2026 Takeaways: ‘One Battle After Another’ Takes Top Honors
item.description: The film won six Academy Awards, including best director and best picture. “Sinners” received four, including for best screenplay and best actor.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The longtime director Paul Thomas Anderson was first nominated for an Oscar in 1998, but he did not win until Sunday night.

item.title: War in Iran Has Grounded Gulf Airlines Like Emirates
item.description: Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad Airways have become some of the world’s largest and most profitable thanks to their location at the center of busy travel routes.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Dubai International Airport early this month. Emirates has become one of the world’s largest and most profitable airlines over the last 40 years.

item.title: It Was Going to Be Magic City Night at the Atlanta Hawks. Then the Outrage Poured In.
item.description: The famous strip club is a symbol of the authentic city to many people in Atlanta. But others wondered whether the N.B.A. should be promoting it.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli and Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: Magic City, a strip club in Atlanta, in 2023. The club has served as a cultural epicenter connecting celebrities, athletes and musicians for decades.

item.title: Waymo Co-CEO Tries to Make a Case for the Safety of Driverless Cars
item.description: Waymo’s co-chief executive, Tekedra Mawakana, knows she needs to earn the public’s trust. It won’t be easy.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Waymo’s co-chief executive, Tekedra Mawakana, said that she understood that the burden was on the company to prove the technology is safe.

item.title: It’s Good to Be a Billionaire, Even at Tax Time
item.description: Paying taxes would feel better if the truly rich were bearing a fair share, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: The $500 Million Mystery Will, Signed by Ghosts
item.description: A seven-page document, mailed by an elusive figure, has set off a court battle over the estate of Tony Hsieh, the former chief executive of Zappos.
item.dc:creator: David Segal

item.title: How to Watch the Oscars 2026: Start Time, Host and Streaming
item.description: Conan O’Brien is back for a second year as the host of the annual awards, which will again be available to stream on Hulu.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times

item.title: The Tax Bill Haunting Your 401(k) and I.R.A.
item.description: Most people have piled their savings into tax-deferred accounts, delaying taxes until they retire. Using Roth options can help ease your tax bite.
item.dc:creator: Mark Miller

item.title: Trump Administration Turns to Migrant Workers to Help Farm Labor Shortage
item.description: As the president’s immigration policies squeeze an already tight supply of farm labor, the Trump administration is making it cheaper to hire foreign farmworkers.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:credit: Rachel Woolf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ernesto Martinez and Noe Rubío, both workers from the H-2A program, pruning peach trees on at Talbott Farms on Monday.

item.title: The Billionaire Backlash Against a Philanthropic Dream
item.description: The Giving Pledge, once trendy among the world’s richest, has come upon hard times.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer

item.title: What Makes ‘Good TV’ on the Internet? Piers Morgan Has Thoughts.
item.description: The provocative broadcaster broke with Fox to build his own company on YouTube. Now he has hired a former head of MSNBC.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Testa
item.media:credit: Tom Jamieson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Piers Morgan in his London studio. His YouTube channel, Uncensored, has more than 4.3 million subscribers.

item.title: For Trump, a Promised Economic Boom Collides With the Costs of War
item.description: President Trump said he had envisioned a growing economy and improving fortunes for American families in 2026. That appears at risk in his war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: By declaring war on Iran, the president has upended his vision for the nation’s economic trajectory.

item.title: California’s Billionaire Tax Battle
item.description: Wealthy residents of the state have put millions of dollars toward stopping a proposed 5 percent tax on their assets.
item.dc:creator: Niko Gallogly
item.media:description: The Google co-founder Sergey Brin has spent $20 million on an effort to stop the proposed wealth tax in California.

item.title: Justice Dept. Legal Threat Complicates Trump’s Pick for Fed Chair
item.description: An investigation of the Federal Reserve was thwarted on Friday, but a department appeal could reimpose an obstacle in Kevin Warsh’s path.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Tony Romm
item.media:description: The path for Kevin M. Warsh to become the Federal Reserve chair may be complicated by an inquiry into renovations at the central bank’s headquarters.

item.title: When Changing Your Address Leads to Losing Your Medicare Coverage
item.description: Using a post office box shouldn’t cost you your health insurance. But in one woman’s case, it sure seemed to.
item.dc:creator: Ron Lieber

item.title: America Depends Less on Oil Than Ever
item.description: Gasoline still drives household budgets, but energy efficiency and renewables have reduced the economy’s overall reliance on petroleum.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Why Little Was Done to Head Off Oil’s Strait of Hormuz Problem
item.description: Geography and regional rivalries have prevented Gulf countries from finding a true alternative to the strait, which the war with Iran has effectively shut down.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sahar Al Attar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A naval vessel sailing this month in the Strait of Hormuz. The waterway remains the only option to export most of the energy produced in the region.

item.title: Fears of ‘Cockroaches’ in the Private Credit Market
item.description: Wall Street figures are turning to colorful metaphors, including invoking the hated insect, to express caution about the $3 trillion risky-lending market.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: When Your Apple Watch Becomes an Office Taskmaster
item.description: A proliferation of data from wearable technology is telling people how to optimize their job performance. Is that a good thing?
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: How Jeff Bezos Upended The Washington Post
item.description: The billionaire newspaper owner, dissatisfied by years of losses, wants the newsroom to double productivity with half its budget.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin, Erik Wemple and Katie Robertson

item.title: Surge in Oil Prices Shakes Pakistan’s Already Fragile Economy
item.description: Families preparing for Eid al-Fitr and farmers ready for harvest are being squeezed in a country that gets nearly all its oil through the Persian Gulf.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Zia ur-Rehman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A fuel storage site in Lahore, Pakistan, on Tuesday.

item.title: China’s Edge in an Oil Shock: Electric Cars and Renewables
item.description: Beijing’s decades-long push to reduce its dependence on foreign oil with huge investments in clean energy sources like electric vehicles is now paying off.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chinese demand for refined oil, gasoline and diesel fell last year as many people in China have switched to electric vehicles.

item.title: TikTok Investors Set to Pay $10 Billion Fee to Trump Administration
item.description: The large fee is the latest example of the White House’s inserting itself into corporate deal making in unusual and aggressive ways.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new investors paid the Treasury roughly $2.5 billion of the fee when the deal closed in January.

item.title: Jared Kushner Solicits Funds for His Firm While Working as Mideast Envoy
item.description: President Trump’s son-in-law is trying to raise $5 billion or more from foreign governments and others for his private equity firm.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Maureen Farrell
item.category: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.media:description: Jared Kushner, the founder of Affinity Partners, at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, in January.

item.title: The Fall of Noma’s René Redzepi Reverberates in the Restaurant World
item.description: In the industry where René Redzepi reigned, fellow chefs are debating how, and how much, restaurant kitchens can change.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin
item.media:credit: Signe Birck for The New York Times
item.media:description: René Redzepi stepped down Wednesday as the chef of Noma, the restaurant that made Copenhagen a global dining destination, after he was accused of past physical abuse.

item.title: Chemical Smell at Control Center Halts Traffic at Washington and Baltimore Airports
item.description: The ground delays, which also affected the airport serving Richmond, Va., were expected to last until at least midnight, according to the F.A.A., which said the smell had affected air traffic controllers.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport was among the airports where the F.A.A. halted flights on Friday afternoon.

item.title: U.S. Vows to Block Iran’s Attempt to Shut Down Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said that the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz were “something we are dealing with.” And about 2,500 Marines were headed to the Middle East to bolster the war effort.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper, Luke Broadwater and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands marched amid airstrikes on Quds Day, a government-sponsored event, on Friday in Tehran.

item.title: Overlooked No More: Eleanor Abbott, the Creator of Candy Land
item.description: She invented the game nearly 80 years ago to distract children who were suffering in the hospital during a polio outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Sandra A. Miller and Margaret Muirhead

item.title: Are You Using A.I. at Work? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: Please tell us how you’re using A.I. tools to fulfill your job responsibilities. We may include your experiences in an article.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Change in Data Sources Led to Lower Inflation Reading
item.description: A methodological change contributed to a better-than-expected inflation report, prompting questions from some economists.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman
item.media:description: Changes to the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index are meaningful to the Federal Reserve, which targets the index for when making policy decisions.

item.title: Judge Quashes Justice Dept.’s Subpoenas of Fed, Crippling Its Pursuit of Trump’s Rivals
item.description: Judge James E. Boasberg derided the U.S. attorney’s office in Washington for pursuing a case against Jerome H. Powell that appeared to be motivated by President Trump’s desire for vengeance.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Glenn Thrush and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The government has offered no evidence whatsoever that Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, committed any crime other than displeasing the president,” Judge James E. Boasberg wrote.

item.title: Trump’s Move to Seize Oil Tankers Costs the U.S. Tens of Millions of Dollars
item.description: Although President Trump said seizing tankers would be a financial boon, the cost of maintaining just one aging ship has already reached $47 million.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:description: The oil tanker Skipper, seized by the United States, seen off the coast of Texas near Galveston in December.

item.title: Lewis Lehrman, 87, Dies; Store Chain Executive Ran for N.Y. Governor
item.description: After helping his family’s Rite-Aid drugstore empire flourish, he waged a surprisingly close but losing race as a Reagan Republican against Mario Cuomo in 1982.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: Gilder Lehrman Institute of American History
item.media:description: Lewis E. Lehrman in October 1982, when he was the Republican candidate for governor in New York. Though a political neophyte, he had one thing the party desperately needed — the money to finance a vibrant campaign.
